The two most significant field hockey tournaments are the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Field hockey at the Summer Olympics") tournament and the [Hockey World Cup](/wiki/Hockey_World_Cup "Hockey World Cup"), both of which are held every four years. Additionally, the [men's](/wiki/Men%27s_FIH_Pro_League "Men's FIH Pro League") and [women's](/wiki/Women%27s_FIH_Pro_League "Women's FIH Pro League") Pro League are held each year for the nine top-ranked teams.

Of the men's teams, Pakistan has won the [Hockey World Cup](/wiki/Men%27s_FIH_Hockey_World_Cup "Men's FIH Hockey World Cup") four times, more times than any other side. India has won gold at the Summer Olympics eight times, including in six successive Olympiads. As for the female teams, the Netherlands has won the [Hockey World Cup](/wiki/Women%27s_FIH_Hockey_World_Cup "Women's FIH Hockey World Cup") the most times, with six titles. At the Olympics, Australia and the Netherlands have both won three Olympic tournaments.

[India](/wiki/India_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "India men's national field hockey team") and [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Pakistan men's national field hockey team") dominated men's hockey until the early 1980s, winning eight Olympic golds and three of the first five world cups, respectively. [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Belgium men's national field hockey team"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Netherlands men's national field hockey team"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Germany men's national field hockey team"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "New Zealand men's national field hockey team"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Australia men's national field hockey team"), and [Spain](/wiki/Spain_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Spain men's national field hockey team") have ascended since the late 1980s, as grass playing surfaces were replaced with artificial turf. Other notable men's nations include [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Argentina men's national field hockey team"), [England](/wiki/England_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "England men's national field hockey team") (who combine with other British "Home Nations" to form the [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Great Britain men's national field hockey team") side at [Olympic events](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Field hockey at the Summer Olympics")) and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "South Korea men's national field hockey team").

The Netherlands, Australia and Argentina are the most successful national teams among women. The [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_women%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Netherlands women's national field hockey team") was the predominant women's team before field hockey was added to Olympic events. In the early 1990s, [Australia](/wiki/Australia_women%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Australia women's national field hockey team") emerged as the strongest women's country, though retirement of a number of players has weakened the team somewhat. [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_women%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Argentina women's national field hockey team") improved its play in the 2000s, heading IFH rankings in 2003, 2010 and 2013. Other prominent women's teams are [Germany](/wiki/Germany_women%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Germany women's national field hockey team"), [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_women%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Great Britain women's national field hockey team"), [China](/wiki/China_women%27s_national_field_hockey_team "China women's national field hockey team"), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_women%27s_national_field_hockey_team "South Korea women's national field hockey team") and [India](/wiki/India_women%27s_national_field_hockey_team "India women's national field hockey team"). Four nations have won [Olympic gold medals in both men's and women's hockey](/wiki/Major_achievements_in_field_hockey_by_nation#Field_hockey_and_indoor_hockey "Major achievements in field hockey by nation"): Germany, Netherlands, Australia and Great Britain.

As of August 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Field_hockey&action=edit) the Dutch lead both men's and women's team FIH world rankings.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

This is a list of the major international field hockey tournaments, in chronological order. Tournaments included are:

* [Olympic Games](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Field hockey at the Summer Olympics") (women's and men's versions) – held every four years.
* [Women's FIH Hockey World Cup](/wiki/Women%27s_FIH_Hockey_World_Cup "Women's FIH Hockey World Cup") and [Men's FIH Hockey World Cup](/wiki/Men%27s_FIH_Hockey_World_Cup "Men's FIH Hockey World Cup") – held every four years, in between the Olympics.
* [Women's FIH Pro League](/wiki/Women%27s_FIH_Pro_League "Women's FIH Pro League") and [Men's FIH Pro League](/wiki/Men%27s_FIH_Pro_League "Men's FIH Pro League") – held annually since 2019, but not in 2020 because of COVID-19.

Defunct tournaments:

* [Champions Trophy](/wiki/Hockey_Champions_Trophy "Hockey Champions Trophy") (women's and men's versions) – both replaced by the Pro Leagues.
* [Champions Challenge](/wiki/Hockey_Champions_Challenge "Hockey Champions Challenge") (women's and men's versions) – both replaced by the World Hockey Leagues.
* [Champions Challenge II](/wiki/Hockey_Champions_Challenge_II "Hockey Champions Challenge II") (women's and men's versions) – both replaced by the World Hockey Leagues.
* [Women's FIH Hockey World League](/wiki/Women%27s_FIH_Hockey_World_League "Women's FIH Hockey World League") and [Men's FIH Hockey World League](/wiki/Men%27s_FIH_Hockey_World_League "Men's FIH Hockey World League") – both replaced by the Pro Leagues.

Other international tournaments include:

* [African Games](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_African_Games "Field hockey at the African Games") – held occasionally since 1987.
* [Asian Games](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_Asian_Games "Field hockey at the Asian Games") – held every four years since 1958.
* [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Hockey_at_the_Commonwealth_Games "Hockey at the Commonwealth Games") – held every four years since 1998 between members of the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations").
* [Pacific Games](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_Pacific_Games "Field hockey at the Pacific Games") – held occasionally since 1979.
* [Pan American Games](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_Pan_American_Games "Field hockey at the Pan American Games") – held every four years since 1967.
* [Sultan Azlan Shah Hockey Tournament](/wiki/Sultan_Azlan_Shah_Hockey_Tournament "Sultan Azlan Shah Hockey Tournament") – held annually in Malaysia, an invitational tournament.
* [Sultan Ibrahim Ismail Hockey Tournament](/wiki/Sultan_of_Johor_Cup "Sultan of Johor Cup") – held annually for athletes aged under-21 in Malaysia, an invitational tournament.

### Indoor hockey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Field_hockey&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Indoor hockey")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Sis%C3%A4hockey_HC_Kilppari.jpg/250px-Sis%C3%A4hockey_HC_Kilppari.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sis%C3%A4hockey_HC_Kilppari.jpg)

[Indoor hockey](/wiki/Indoor_hockey "Indoor hockey") match at Samppalinna Ball Game Hall in [Turku, Finland](/wiki/Turku "Turku"), 2016

A popular variant of field hockey is [indoor hockey](/wiki/Indoor_hockey "Indoor hockey"), which is 6-a-side (5-a-side during 2014–2015) using a field which is reduced to approximately 40 m × 20 m (131 ft × 66 ft). Although many of the rules remain the same, including obstruction and feet, there are several key variations: players may not raise the ball unless shooting at goal, players may not hit the ball, instead using pushes to transfer it, and the sidelines are replaced with solid barriers, from which the ball will rebound and remain in play.[[40]](#cite_note-International_Hockey_Federation-40) In addition, the regulation guidelines for the indoor hockey stick require a slightly thinner, lighter stick than an outdoor one.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

### Hockey5s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Field_hockey&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Hockey5s")]

Hockey5s

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Highest [governing body](/wiki/Sports_governing_body "Sports governing body") | [International Hockey Federation](/wiki/International_Hockey_Federation "International Hockey Federation") |
| Characteristics | |
| Contact | Yes |
| Team members | 5 per side (30 Minutes in 2 period) |
| Type | Hockey5s |
| Equipment | [hockey ball](/wiki/Hockey_ball "Hockey ball"), [hockey stick](/wiki/Hockey_stick "Hockey stick"), [mouthguard](/wiki/Mouthguard "Mouthguard"), gloves, [shinpads](/wiki/Shin_guards "Shin guards") |
| Presence | |
| [Olympic](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") | No |
| [World Championships](/wiki/World_championship "World championship") | [2024](/wiki/2024_Hockey5s_World_Cup "2024 Hockey5s World Cup") |
| [World Games](/wiki/World_Games "World Games") | No |

As the name suggests, Hockey5s is a hockey variant which features five players on each team (including a goalkeeper). The field of play is 55 m long and 41.70 m wide—this is approximately half the size of a [regular pitch](/wiki/Field_hockey_pitch "Field hockey pitch"). Few additional markings are needed as there is no penalty circle nor penalty corners; shots can be taken from anywhere on the pitch. Penalty strokes are replaced by a "challenge" which is like the one-on-one method used in a [penalty shoot-out](/wiki/Penalty_shoot-out_(field_hockey) "Penalty shoot-out (field hockey)"). The duration of the match is three 12-minute periods with an interval of two minutes between periods; [golden goal](/wiki/Golden_goal "Golden goal") periods are multiple 5-minute periods. The rules are simpler and it is intended that the game is faster, creating more shots on goal with less play in midfield, and more attractive to spectators.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

An Asian qualification tournament for two places at the [2014 Youth Olympic Games](/wiki/2014_Summer_Youth_Olympics "2014 Summer Youth Olympics") was the first time an FIH event used the Hockey5s format.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Hockey5s was also used for the [Pacific Games](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_Pacific_Games "Field hockey at the Pacific Games") in 2015[[58]](#cite_note-58) and at the [African Youth Games](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_2018_African_Youth_Games "Field hockey at the 2018 African Youth Games") in 2018.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

In 2022, the FIH staged its first senior international Hockey5s event, with a [men's](/wiki/2022_Men%27s_FIH_Hockey5s "2022 Men's FIH Hockey5s") and [women's](/wiki/2022_Women%27s_FIH_Hockey5s "2022 Women's FIH Hockey5s") event being held in [Lausanne](/wiki/Lausanne "Lausanne").[[60]](#cite_note-60) The [FIH Men's Hockey5s World Cup](/wiki/FIH_Men%27s_Hockey5s_World_Cup "FIH Men's Hockey5s World Cup") and [FIH Women's Hockey5s World Cup](/wiki/FIH_Women%27s_Hockey5s_World_Cup "FIH Women's Hockey5s World Cup") are set to debut in 2024.
